findings.title: burnFrom + mint Combination Allows totalSupply Invariant Violation
findings.description: Since both mint() and burnFrom() are completely unrestricted (no access control), any attacker can: (1) call burnFrom() on any victim's balance to reduce totalSupply below its true value, then (2) call mint() up to maxSupply again. This allows an attacker to effectively steal tokens from any holder by burning their balance without allowance, and simultaneously re-mint to themselves. The invariant totalSupply == sum(balanceOf[all addresses]) can be temporarily maintained but user funds are destroyed arbitrarily.

findings.title: Unrestricted Mint Function Allows Anyone to Mint Tokens
findings.description: The mint() function lacks any access control check. Any external address can call mint() to create tokens up to the maxSupply cap. The comment explicitly notes 'Missing: require(msg.sender == owner)'. This completely breaks token supply integrity.
findings.message: Any attacker can mint up to 900,000 additional tokens (maxSupply - initial 100,000) to any address of their choice, effectively stealing value from all existing holders through dilution, or acquiring tokens for free to sell on exchanges.
findings.recommendation: Add owner-only access control: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'); at the start of mint(). Consider also using OpenZeppelin's Ownable or AccessControl patterns.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Deploy UnsafeToken
2. Attacker calls mint(attackerAddress, 900000 * 10**18)
3. Attacker now holds 900,000 tokens they did not pay for
4. Attacker sells tokens on DEX, draining the liquidity pool

findings.title: Unrestricted burnFrom Allows Anyone to Burn Any User's Tokens
findings.description: The burnFrom() function has no authorization check. It does not verify that msg.sender is the token owner, has an allowance, or is a privileged role. Any caller can specify any 'from' address and burn their entire balance without consent.
findings.message: An attacker can burn the token balance of any address, including the owner, liquidity pools, or any user. This can be used for targeted griefing, price manipulation (burning LP tokens), or sabotaging specific accounts.
findings.recommendation: Add allowance-based authorization: require(allowance[from][msg.sender] >= amount, 'Insufficient allowance'); and then decrement the allowance. Alternatively restrict to owner only if that is the intended design.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Alice holds 50,000 tokens
2. Attacker calls burnFrom(alice, 50000 * 10**18)
3. Alice's balance is reduced to zero with no authorization from Alice
4. Attacker can combine with mint() to steal supply: burn tokens from large holders, mint to own address

findings.title: Arbitrary External Call in transferWithCallback Enables Reentrancy and Code Execution
findings.description: The transferWithCallback() function performs an arbitrary low-level call to the recipient address with attacker-controlled calldata after transferring tokens. The return value of the external call is not checked. Furthermore, the call happens after state changes (transfer), but an attacker can use the callback to re-enter transferWithCallback() or other state-changing functions. The combination of arbitrary calldata + unchecked return value creates a severe attack surface.
findings.message: 1. Reentrancy: attacker contract receives callback and re-enters transfer/mint functions before state is finalized. 2. Arbitrary code execution on any contract: attacker can craft calldata to call any function on any contract through this relay. 3. Since return value is ignored, silent failures go undetected.
findings.recommendation: Apply Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern: update state BEFORE external calls. Use OpenZeppelin's ReentrancyGuard (`nonReentrant` modifier).
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker deploys a malicious contract with a fallback/receive function that calls back into the victim.
2. Attacker calls the victim's withdraw() function with a valid balance.
3. Victim sends ETH to attacker — attacker's fallback immediately calls withdraw() again before state is updated.
4. Re-entry loop drains the contract balance in a single transaction (no per-call state update blocks it).
5. Attack ends when gas runs out or balance is exhausted; attacker keeps all drained ETH.

findings.message: Weak Randomness
findings.description: The use of keccak256 hash functions on predictable values like block.timestamp, block.number, or similar data, including modulo operations on these values, should be avoided for generating randomness, as they are easily predictable and manipulable. The `PREVRANDAO` opcode also should not be used as a source of randomness. Instead, utilize Chainlink VRF for cryptographically secure and provably random values to ensure protocol integrity.
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal scheme for randomness
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the on-chain randomness source (blockhash, block.timestamp, block.difficulty).
2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.
3. Attacker submits their exploit transaction in the same block as or immediately after the target, controlling the outcome.
4. For lottery contracts: attacker only submits when they know they will win; reverts otherwise (cost: only gas).
5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: UnsafeToken.timeLock() (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#110-113) uses a weak PRNG: "block.timestamp % 2 == 0 (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#112)"
findings.description: [UnsafeToken.timeLock()](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L110-L113) uses a weak PRNG: "[block.timestamp % 2 == 0](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L112)"
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink VRF for verifiable randomness. Never use `block.timestamp`, `blockhash`, or `block.difficulty` as entropy sources.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the on-chain randomness source (blockhash, block.timestamp, block.difficulty).
2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.
3. Attacker submits their exploit transaction in the same block as or immediately after the target, controlling the outcome.
4. For lottery contracts: attacker only submits when they know they will win; reverts otherwise (cost: only gas).
5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256) (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#116-125) uses a weak PRNG: "random = uint256(keccak256(bytes)(abi.encodePacked(block.timestamp,block.prevrandao,msg.sender))) % 100 (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#118-122)"
findings.description: [UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256)](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L116-L125) uses a weak PRNG: "[random = uint256(keccak256(bytes)(abi.encodePacked(block.timestamp,block.prevrandao,msg.sender))) % 100](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L118-L122)"
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink VRF for verifiable randomness. Never use `block.timestamp`, `blockhash`, or `block.difficulty` as entropy sources.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the on-chain randomness source (blockhash, block.timestamp, block.difficulty).
2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.
3. Attacker submits their exploit transaction in the same block as or immediately after the target, controlling the outcome.
4. For lottery contracts: attacker only submits when they know they will win; reverts otherwise (cost: only gas).
5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: Return value of the function call is not checked.
findings.description: Function returns a value but it is ignored.
findings.recommendation: Always check return value of low-level calls or use SafeERC20
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Weak Randomness
findings.description: The use of keccak256 hash functions on predictable values like block.timestamp, block.number, or similar data, including modulo operations on these values, should be avoided for generating randomness, as they are easily predictable and manipulable. The `PREVRANDAO` opcode also should not be used as a source of randomness. Instead, utilize Chainlink VRF for cryptographically secure and provably random values to ensure protocol integrity.
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal scheme for randomness
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the on-chain randomness source (blockhash, block.timestamp, block.difficulty).
2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.
3. Attacker submits their exploit transaction in the same block as or immediately after the target, controlling the outcome.
4. For lottery contracts: attacker only submits when they know they will win; reverts otherwise (cost: only gas).
5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Paused State Not Enforced on Any Transfer Function
findings.description: The contract has a paused state variable and pause()/unpause() admin functions, but none of the token transfer functions (transfer, transferFrom, transferWithCallback, batchTransfer, mint, burnFrom) check require(!paused). The pause mechanism is completely non-functional and provides false security assurance.
findings.message: The owner cannot pause the contract in emergency situations (e.g., exploit in progress, hack detected). All token transfers, mints, and burns will continue regardless of the paused state, making incident response impossible.
findings.recommendation: Add require(!paused, 'Contract is paused') to transfer(), transferFrom(), mint(), burnFrom(), transferWithCallback(), and batchTransfer(). Consider using a modifier: modifier whenNotPaused() { require(!paused, 'Paused'); _; }
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Owner discovers an active exploit and calls pause()
2. paused is set to true
3. Attacker continues calling mint(), burnFrom(), transfer() — all succeed because no function checks paused
4. Owner has no way to stop the attack

findings.title: Unbounded Loop in batchTransfer Causes DoS and Drains Caller
findings.description: batchTransfer() iterates over an unbounded recipients array without any length cap. For very large arrays, the transaction will exceed the block gas limit and revert. Additionally, the function transfers 'amount' per recipient but does not pre-check that msg.sender has sufficient balance for ALL transfers (balanceOf[msg.sender] >= amount * recipients.length). Early transfers succeed while later ones revert, resulting in partial execution with inconsistent state.
findings.message: 1. DoS: transactions with large recipient arrays always revert, wasting gas. 2. Partial execution: if balance runs out mid-loop, some transfers succeed and others revert — but since each transfer() call is separate, the partial state IS committed (each sub-call modifies state). Actually in Solidity, since require in transfer() will revert the entire transaction on failure, this causes total revert. However an attacker could craft arrays where gas runs out silently mid-execution.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add a maximum batch size: require(recipients.length <= 200, 'Batch too large'). 2. Pre-check total balance: require(balanceOf[msg.sender] >= amount * recipients.length, 'Insufficient balance for batch'). 3. Consider using SafeMath-style overflow check on the multiplication.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker calls batchTransfer with 100,000 recipients
2. Transaction runs out of gas mid-loop
3. Entire transaction reverts but caller wasted significant gas
4. Alternatively: legitimate user with 1000 tokens calls batchTransfer([addr1..addr10], 200) — fails on addr6 when balance hits 0, entire batch reverts unexpectedly

findings.title: Missing Zero Address Check Allows Permanent Token Loss
findings.description: The transfer() and transferFrom() functions do not check that the 'to' address is not address(0). Tokens sent to address(0) are permanently burned without updating totalSupply, violating the invariant that totalSupply == sum of all balanceOf values. The zero address has no private key and tokens sent there are unrecoverable.
findings.message: Users who accidentally or intentionally send tokens to address(0) permanently lose them. The totalSupply remains inflated relative to actual circulating supply, causing accounting inconsistencies. This breaks the core ERC20 invariant.
findings.recommendation: Add to transfer() and transferFrom(): require(to != address(0), 'Transfer to zero address'); Also consider adding require(from != address(0)) in transferFrom().
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User calls transfer(address(0), 1000 * 10**18) by mistake
2. balanceOf[address(0)] increases by 1000 tokens
3. totalSupply remains unchanged but 1000 tokens are permanently inaccessible
4. Protocol accounting is corrupted

findings.title: Locked Ether with No Withdrawal Function
findings.description: The contract implements a receive() payable function that accepts ETH, but there is no withdraw function, no emergency rescue function, and no way to recover sent ETH. Any ETH sent to this contract is permanently locked.
findings.message: Any ETH accidentally or intentionally sent to this contract is permanently lost. If this contract is used as a DEX liquidity token or interacts with protocols that send ETH, those funds are unrecoverable. Total value locked is permanently frozen.
findings.recommendation: Either remove the receive() function entirely if ETH is not needed, or add a withdrawal function: function withdrawETH() external { require(msg.sender == owner); (bool success,) = owner.call{value: address(this).balance}(''); require(success); }
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User accidentally sends 10 ETH to UnsafeToken address
2. ETH is accepted by receive() and credited to contract balance
3. No function exists to withdraw ETH
4. 10 ETH is permanently locked

findings.title: Approve Race Condition Allows Double-Spend of Allowance
findings.description: The standard ERC20 approve() race condition: when an owner changes a spender's allowance from N to M, the spender can observe the pending transaction and front-run it to spend the original N tokens before the new allowance is set, then spend M more after. This results in the spender consuming N+M tokens instead of M.
findings.message: A malicious or front-running spender can extract up to N+M tokens when the owner intended to change allowance from N to M. On networks with public mempools (e.g., Ethereum mainnet), this is reliably exploitable.
findings.recommendation: Add increaseAllowance() and decreaseAllowance() functions. Alternatively, require the current allowance to be 0 before setting a new non-zero value: require(amount == 0 || allowance[msg.sender][spender] == 0, 'Reset allowance to 0 first').
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Alice approves Bob for 100 tokens (allowance = 100)
2. Alice decides to change allowance to 50, submits approve(bob, 50)
3. Bob sees the pending transaction in mempool
4. Bob front-runs with transferFrom(alice, bob, 100) — spends original 100
5. Alice's approve(bob, 50) confirms, setting allowance to 50
6. Bob calls transferFrom(alice, bob, 50) again — spends 50 more
7. Bob has extracted 150 tokens instead of 50

findings.description: On-chain randomness source is predictable — use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal.
findings.recommendation: On-chain randomness source is predictable — use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal.

2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.

5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: Define and use `constant` variables instead of using literals
findings.description: If the same constant literal value is used multiple times, create a constant state variable and reference it throughout the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: `public` functions not used internally could be marked `external`
findings.description: Instead of marking a function as `public`, consider marking it as `external` if it is not used internally.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: `public` functions not used internally could be marked `external`
findings.description: Instead of marking a function as `public`, consider marking it as `external` if it is not used internally.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: `public` functions not used internally could be marked `external`
findings.description: Instead of marking a function as `public`, consider marking it as `external` if it is not used internally.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256) (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#116-125) uses a dangerous strict equality:
findings.description:
[UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256)](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L116-L125) uses a dangerous strict equality:
	- [guess == random](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L124)
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the vulnerability

findings.message: UnsafeToken.timeLock() (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#110-113) uses a dangerous strict equality:

findings.recommendation: Review and fix the vulnerability

findings.message: Contract locking ether found:
findings.description:
Contract locking ether found:
	Contract [UnsafeToken](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L9-L142) has payable functions:
	 - [UnsafeToken.receive()](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L139-L141)
	But does not have a function to withdraw the ether
findings.recommendation: Provide a withdrawal function or ensure contract is payable

findings.message: `public` functions not used internally could be marked `external`
findings.description: Instead of marking a function as `public`, consider marking it as `external` if it is not used internally.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: `public` functions not used internally could be marked `external`
findings.description: Instead of marking a function as `public`, consider marking it as `external` if it is not used internally.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Loop bounded by dynamic array length — may exceed block gas limit on large arrays. Consider pagination.
findings.description: Loop bounded by dynamic array length — may exceed block gas limit on large arrays. Consider pagination.
findings.recommendation: Loop bounded by dynamic array length — may exceed block gas limit on large arrays. Consider pagination.

findings.message: Block timestamp/number used — manipulable by miners (±15 seconds).
findings.description: Block timestamp/number used — manipulable by miners (±15 seconds).
findings.recommendation: Block timestamp/number used — manipulable by miners (±15 seconds).
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract uses block.timestamp to enforce a time lock or deadline (e.g. 'require(block.timestamp >= unlock)').
2. Miner (or validator in PoS) can adjust block.timestamp within the ~15-second tolerance window.
3. Attacker who is also a miner sets the timestamp slightly ahead to satisfy the time condition prematurely.
4. Attacker calls the time-locked function before the intended unlock time, bypassing the control.
5. For auctions or games: manipulating the timestamp extends or shortens windows to benefit the attacker.

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256) (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#116-125) uses timestamp for comparisons
findings.description:
[UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256)](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L116-L125) uses timestamp for comparisons
	Dangerous comparisons:
	- [guess == random](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L124)
findings.recommendation: Avoid relying on `block.timestamp` for critical logic — miners can manipulate by ±15 seconds. Use block numbers for ordering if precision needed.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract uses block.timestamp to enforce a time lock or deadline (e.g. 'require(block.timestamp >= unlock)').
2. Miner (or validator in PoS) can adjust block.timestamp within the ~15-second tolerance window.
3. Attacker who is also a miner sets the timestamp slightly ahead to satisfy the time condition prematurely.
4. Attacker calls the time-locked function before the intended unlock time, bypassing the control.
5. For auctions or games: manipulating the timestamp extends or shortens windows to benefit the attacker.

exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the on-chain randomness source (blockhash, block.timestamp, block.difficulty).
2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.
3. Attacker submits their exploit transaction in the same block as or immediately after the target, controlling the outcome.
4. For lottery contracts: attacker only submits when they know they will win; reverts otherwise (cost: only gas).
5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256) (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#116-125) uses a weak PRNG: "random = uint256(keccak256(bytes)(abi.encodePacked(block.timestamp,block.prevrandao,msg.sender))) % 100 (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#118-122)"
findings.description: [UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256)](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L116-L125) uses a weak PRNG: "[random = uint256(keccak256(bytes)(abi.encodePacked(block.timestamp,block.prevrandao,msg.sender))) % 100](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L118-L122)"
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink VRF for verifiable randomness. Never use `block.timestamp`, `blockhash`, or `block.difficulty` as entropy sources.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the on-chain randomness source (blockhash, block.timestamp, block.difficulty).
2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.
3. Attacker submits their exploit transaction in the same block as or immediately after the target, controlling the outcome.
4. For lottery contracts: attacker only submits when they know they will win; reverts otherwise (cost: only gas).
5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: Return value of the function call is not checked.
findings.description: Function returns a value but it is ignored.
findings.recommendation: Always check return value of low-level calls or use SafeERC20
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Weak Randomness
findings.description: The use of keccak256 hash functions on predictable values like block.timestamp, block.number, or similar data, including modulo operations on these values, should be avoided for generating randomness, as they are easily predictable and manipulable. The `PREVRANDAO` opcode also should not be used as a source of randomness. Instead, utilize Chainlink VRF for cryptographically secure and provably random values to ensure protocol integrity.
findings.recommendation: Use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal scheme for randomness
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the on-chain randomness source (blockhash, block.timestamp, block.difficulty).
2. Before the target transaction is mined, attacker simulates possible block values to predict the 'random' outcome.
3. Attacker submits their exploit transaction in the same block as or immediately after the target, controlling the outcome.
4. For lottery contracts: attacker only submits when they know they will win; reverts otherwise (cost: only gas).
5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: Contract locks Ether without a withdraw function.
findings.description: It appears that the contract includes a payable function to accept Ether but lacks a corresponding function to withdraw it, which leads to the Ether being locked in the contract. To resolve this issue, please implement a public or external function that allows for the withdrawal of Ether from the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.title: Paused State Not Enforced on Any Transfer Function
findings.description: The contract has a paused state variable and pause()/unpause() admin functions, but none of the token transfer functions (transfer, transferFrom, transferWithCallback, batchTransfer, mint, burnFrom) check require(!paused). The pause mechanism is completely non-functional and provides false security assurance.
findings.message: The owner cannot pause the contract in emergency situations (e.g., exploit in progress, hack detected). All token transfers, mints, and burns will continue regardless of the paused state, making incident response impossible.
findings.recommendation: Add require(!paused, 'Contract is paused') to transfer(), transferFrom(), mint(), burnFrom(), transferWithCallback(), and batchTransfer(). Consider using a modifier: modifier whenNotPaused() { require(!paused, 'Paused'); _; }
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Owner discovers an active exploit and calls pause()
2. paused is set to true
3. Attacker continues calling mint(), burnFrom(), transfer() — all succeed because no function checks paused
4. Owner has no way to stop the attack

findings.title: Unbounded Loop in batchTransfer Causes DoS and Drains Caller
findings.description: batchTransfer() iterates over an unbounded recipients array without any length cap. For very large arrays, the transaction will exceed the block gas limit and revert. Additionally, the function transfers 'amount' per recipient but does not pre-check that msg.sender has sufficient balance for ALL transfers (balanceOf[msg.sender] >= amount * recipients.length). Early transfers succeed while later ones revert, resulting in partial execution with inconsistent state.
findings.message: 1. DoS: transactions with large recipient arrays always revert, wasting gas. 2. Partial execution: if balance runs out mid-loop, some transfers succeed and others revert — but since each transfer() call is separate, the partial state IS committed (each sub-call modifies state). Actually in Solidity, since require in transfer() will revert the entire transaction on failure, this causes total revert. However an attacker could craft arrays where gas runs out silently mid-execution.
findings.recommendation: 1. Add a maximum batch size: require(recipients.length <= 200, 'Batch too large'). 2. Pre-check total balance: require(balanceOf[msg.sender] >= amount * recipients.length, 'Insufficient balance for batch'). 3. Consider using SafeMath-style overflow check on the multiplication.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker calls batchTransfer with 100,000 recipients
2. Transaction runs out of gas mid-loop
3. Entire transaction reverts but caller wasted significant gas
4. Alternatively: legitimate user with 1000 tokens calls batchTransfer([addr1..addr10], 200) — fails on addr6 when balance hits 0, entire batch reverts unexpectedly

findings.title: Missing Zero Address Check Allows Permanent Token Loss
findings.description: The transfer() and transferFrom() functions do not check that the 'to' address is not address(0). Tokens sent to address(0) are permanently burned without updating totalSupply, violating the invariant that totalSupply == sum of all balanceOf values. The zero address has no private key and tokens sent there are unrecoverable.
findings.message: Users who accidentally or intentionally send tokens to address(0) permanently lose them. The totalSupply remains inflated relative to actual circulating supply, causing accounting inconsistencies. This breaks the core ERC20 invariant.
findings.recommendation: Add to transfer() and transferFrom(): require(to != address(0), 'Transfer to zero address'); Also consider adding require(from != address(0)) in transferFrom().
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User calls transfer(address(0), 1000 * 10**18) by mistake
2. balanceOf[address(0)] increases by 1000 tokens
3. totalSupply remains unchanged but 1000 tokens are permanently inaccessible
4. Protocol accounting is corrupted

findings.title: Locked Ether with No Withdrawal Function
findings.description: The contract implements a receive() payable function that accepts ETH, but there is no withdraw function, no emergency rescue function, and no way to recover sent ETH. Any ETH sent to this contract is permanently locked.
findings.message: Any ETH accidentally or intentionally sent to this contract is permanently lost. If this contract is used as a DEX liquidity token or interacts with protocols that send ETH, those funds are unrecoverable. Total value locked is permanently frozen.
findings.recommendation: Either remove the receive() function entirely if ETH is not needed, or add a withdrawal function: function withdrawETH() external { require(msg.sender == owner); (bool success,) = owner.call{value: address(this).balance}(''); require(success); }
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User accidentally sends 10 ETH to UnsafeToken address
2. ETH is accepted by receive() and credited to contract balance
3. No function exists to withdraw ETH
4. 10 ETH is permanently locked

findings.title: Approve Race Condition Allows Double-Spend of Allowance
findings.description: The standard ERC20 approve() race condition: when an owner changes a spender's allowance from N to M, the spender can observe the pending transaction and front-run it to spend the original N tokens before the new allowance is set, then spend M more after. This results in the spender consuming N+M tokens instead of M.
findings.message: A malicious or front-running spender can extract up to N+M tokens when the owner intended to change allowance from N to M. On networks with public mempools (e.g., Ethereum mainnet), this is reliably exploitable.
findings.recommendation: Add increaseAllowance() and decreaseAllowance() functions. Alternatively, require the current allowance to be 0 before setting a new non-zero value: require(amount == 0 || allowance[msg.sender][spender] == 0, 'Reset allowance to 0 first').
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Alice approves Bob for 100 tokens (allowance = 100)
2. Alice decides to change allowance to 50, submits approve(bob, 50)
3. Bob sees the pending transaction in mempool
4. Bob front-runs with transferFrom(alice, bob, 100) — spends original 100
5. Alice's approve(bob, 50) confirms, setting allowance to 50
6. Bob calls transferFrom(alice, bob, 50) again — spends 50 more
7. Bob has extracted 150 tokens instead of 50

findings.message: On-chain randomness source is predictable — use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal.
findings.description: On-chain randomness source is predictable — use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal.
findings.recommendation: On-chain randomness source is predictable — use Chainlink VRF or commit-reveal.

5. Attacker drains the prize pool repeatedly with a guaranteed-win strategy.

findings.message: Define and use `constant` variables instead of using literals
findings.description: If the same constant literal value is used multiple times, create a constant state variable and reference it throughout the contract.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: `public` functions not used internally could be marked `external`
findings.description: Instead of marking a function as `public`, consider marking it as `external` if it is not used internally.

findings.message: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.description: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: Low-level call without explicit success check — use require(success) or SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: Loop bounded by dynamic array length — may exceed block gas limit on large arrays. Consider pagination.
findings.description: Loop bounded by dynamic array length — may exceed block gas limit on large arrays. Consider pagination.
findings.recommendation: Loop bounded by dynamic array length — may exceed block gas limit on large arrays. Consider pagination.

findings.message: Block timestamp/number used — manipulable by miners (±15 seconds).
findings.description: Block timestamp/number used — manipulable by miners (±15 seconds).
findings.recommendation: Block timestamp/number used — manipulable by miners (±15 seconds).
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract uses block.timestamp to enforce a time lock or deadline (e.g. 'require(block.timestamp >= unlock)').
2. Miner (or validator in PoS) can adjust block.timestamp within the ~15-second tolerance window.
3. Attacker who is also a miner sets the timestamp slightly ahead to satisfy the time condition prematurely.
4. Attacker calls the time-locked function before the intended unlock time, bypassing the control.
5. For auctions or games: manipulating the timestamp extends or shortens windows to benefit the attacker.

findings.message: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.description: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
findings.recommendation: Approval pattern susceptible to front-running race condition. Consider increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance.
exploit_scenario: 1. Victim calls token.approve(spender, 100) to change an existing approval from 50 to 100.
2. Attacker monitors the mempool and sees the approve transaction before it is mined.
3. Attacker front-runs by calling transferFrom(victim, attacker, 50) while the old allowance of 50 is still active.
4. After the victim's approve is mined, attacker calls transferFrom again using the new allowance of 100.
5. Attacker steals 150 tokens even though the victim intended to grant only 100.
6. Fix: use increaseAllowance/decreaseAllowance which are atomic and race-free.

findings.message: UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256) (../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#116-125) uses timestamp for comparisons
findings.description:
[UnsafeToken.lottery(uint256)](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L116-L125) uses timestamp for comparisons
	Dangerous comparisons:
	- [guess == random](../data/audit/UnsafeToken.sol#L124)
findings.recommendation: Avoid relying on `block.timestamp` for critical logic — miners can manipulate by ±15 seconds. Use block numbers for ordering if precision needed.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract uses block.timestamp to enforce a time lock or deadline (e.g. 'require(block.timestamp >= unlock)').
2. Miner (or validator in PoS) can adjust block.timestamp within the ~15-second tolerance window.
3. Attacker who is also a miner sets the timestamp slightly ahead to satisfy the time condition prematurely.
4. Attacker calls the time-locked function before the intended unlock time, bypassing the control.
5. For auctions or games: manipulating the timestamp extends or shortens windows to benefit the attacker.
